# Fillable PDF Implementation Notes

**Scope:** Initial Evaluation Checklist prototype (`downloads/Initial_Evaluation_Checklist_Fillable.pdf`), generated by a new "Download Fillable PDF" button in the Hub's Downloads panel.

## How it works

The Hub now vendors [pdf-lib](https://github.com/Hopding/pdf-lib) v1.17.1 (MIT license) inline as a `<script>` block, immediately before the Hub's own script. This is a real PDF-construction library — it builds a genuine AcroForm PDF (the same form-field standard used by essentially every PDF viewer), not a rasterized print-out. Clicking "Download Fillable PDF":

1. Runs entirely in the browser tab. No network request is made, and no data is sent anywhere — the PDF bytes are constructed in memory and handed to the browser's native download mechanism (`Blob` + object URL).
2. Reads the checklist's live data (`CHECKLISTS.find(c => c.id === 'initial')`) — same source of truth as the interactive Checklists tab — so the PDF can never drift out of sync with the in-Hub content.
3. Draws a coordinate-based layout (header bar, info fields, "Most Common Misses" callout, checklist groups with checkboxes, notes, sign-off) using a small reusable `newPdfWriter()` engine that auto-paginates when content runs long.
4. On any failure, catches the error and shows a visible, dismissible banner ("Couldn't generate the PDF…") rather than failing silently — verified by simulating a load failure.

## Field types used (and why)

| Field type | Used for | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Text field | Student Name, District, dates, Evaluator, Completed by, Signature | Universally supported AcroForm field — renders and fills identically everywhere |
| Multiline text field | Notes | Same field type with `.enableMultiline()` — no special viewer support needed |
| Checkbox | All 15 checklist items | Native AcroForm checkbox widget |

No dropdowns were needed for this document (nothing in it is a fixed short list). No date-picker or signature-widget fields were used — per your instruction, dates and signatures are plain text fields (`MM/DD/YYYY` is labeled directly on the field).

## What was deliberately left out, and why

Per your direction, **zero embedded PDF JavaScript** was used anywhere in the document. That means:

- No auto-calculated completion percentage
- No auto-checking of sections
- No dynamic/conditional tooltips

These all require PDF JavaScript, which only executes reliably in Adobe Acrobat Reader. Chrome PDF Viewer, Edge PDF Viewer, and macOS Preview either ignore AcroForm JavaScript entirely or support only a limited subset — shipping it would mean the form behaves differently depending on which app opens it, which is exactly what you asked me to avoid.

## Cross-viewer support matrix (what actually works where)

| Feature | Acrobat Reader | Chrome PDF Viewer | Edge PDF Viewer | macOS Preview |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Text field fill & save | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Multiline text field | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Checkbox check/uncheck & save | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Field values persist when the PDF is saved/reopened | ✅ | ✅ (via "Save as" / print-to-PDF preserves values in most current versions) | ✅ | ✅ |
| Tab order follows field creation order | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Partial — Preview's form-field tab order support is the least consistent of the four |
| Field name as accessible label | ✅ | Partial | Partial | Partial |
| Static colored borders (visual "required field" cue) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| PDF JavaScript (calculations, auto-check, conditional tooltips) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |

Everything actually shipped in this prototype is a ✅-row across all four viewers. The only ❌ row (JavaScript) is the row we intentionally didn't use.

## Accessibility approach taken

- Every field has a clear, human-readable field name (e.g., `Student_Name_1`, not `field1`) — this is what most screen readers and Acrobat's field-navigation UI surface as the field's identity, since not all viewers reliably expose a separate tooltip/alt-text string.
- A visible on-page caption sits directly above every field (this doubles as both the visual label and the fallback for viewers with weaker accessible-name support).
- Fields are created in top-to-bottom, left-to-right order, which determines tab order in practice across viewers.
- Full PDF/UA structural tagging (a more rigorous accessibility standard involving a tagged content tree) was not attempted — see the companion limitations document.

## Branding fidelity

Colors were replicated exactly from the Hub's CSS custom properties (teal header `#0F7368`, orange "Most Common Misses" callout, red "HIGH-RISK" tags, subtle gray field borders with a slightly bolder teal border on required fields). Typography uses PDF's built-in Helvetica/Helvetica-Bold/Helvetica-Oblique fonts rather than the Hub's Fraunces/Inter/IBM Plex Mono web fonts — see limitations document for why.
